content.text: A type annotation tells the TypeScript compiler the kind of value a variable, parameter, or return site can hold. Annotations are written after a colon —
— and are completely erased before the code reaches the browser. They exist only to help the compiler check your code and power IDE tooling.

attrs.alt: TypeScript type hierarchy — from unknown at the top to never at the bottom

content.text: TypeScript's primitive types map directly to JavaScript's primitive values. Annotations here are often optional because TypeScript infers them from initialiser values, but they become required on uninitialized variables and function parameters.
are not assignable to other types unless you include them in a union. This prevents a whole class of runtime errors.
is shorter and the Angular convention.
A tuple is a fixed-length array where each position has its own type. Tuples are common when returning multiple values from a function.
Object types describe the shape of a value — its property names and their types. You can write them inline or give them a name with an interface or type alias.
Interfaces give a reusable name to an object type. They can be extended (
), declared multiple times (declaration merging), and used as the type for class instances.

keyword creates a named alias for any type expression — primitives, unions, intersections, tuples, mapped types, and conditional types. Unlike interfaces, a type alias cannot be reopened after declaration.
A union type accepts values of
of the listed types. TypeScript narrows the union inside conditional branches.
An intersection type requires the value to satisfy
of the combined types simultaneously — useful for mixing behaviours.
Literal Types and Template Literal Types
A literal type narrows a value to exactly one specific value. When combined in unions, they form a closed set of allowed values — essentially a typesafe enum.
Angular uses TypeScript types throughout its API — component inputs and outputs, service method signatures, dependency injection tokens, and template types.
TypeScript ships with built-in utility types that transform an existing type into a new one. They reduce boilerplate and keep derived types in sync with their source automatically.
Object with keys K and values V
Keep only U from union T
operator produces a union of a type's known property names. It keeps property access type-safe — you can only pass a key that actually exists on the type.
keyword in a type position (not inside a condition) extracts the inferred type of a value. It is useful when the type is complex but can be inferred from an existing value.
A mapped type iterates over the keys of a type and transforms them. The built-in utility types like
) selects a type branch depending on whether
. They are used in advanced library types and Angular's
Different positions in TypeScript code require or accept type annotations in specific places.
Items can hold anything; no IDE help
is the class constructor, not a type
— TS flags prop may be undefined

Type annotations are the foundation of TypeScript's type system. They describe what a variable, parameter, or return value can be, and the compiler enforces those constraints across the entire program.
In Angular, annotate all @Input, @Output, service method parameters and return types explicitly — it documents the component contract and catches mistakes before runtime
